body:
With the new Trust Model (grades S/A/B/C) fully implemented, we need to enforce a gate that ties a skill's general rank progression (e.g., Evolved, Apex) directly to its Overall Trust Grade.

Currently, we enforce the ultimate gate (pillar rule) and verification workflow gates (Issue #658 for Enterprise Ready), but general rank ascension does not yet strictly require specific evidence grade minimums. This issue tracks updating the meta logic in `meta.json` and the `grading.py` pipeline to enforce these rank gates (e.g., reaching Evolved requires an Overall Grade of ≥ B, Apex requires ≥ A).
title: Feature: Fully Set Up Gate on Evidence Grades and Ranking

title: Implement Rising Repositories View

body: Create frontend views to display Recently Ascended skills. Should highlight rank changes, newly named skills, and new apex skills based on the trending engine data.
title: Implement Rising Skills View

body: Build the backend logic to track and serve trending metrics for 24h, 7d, and 30d views. Metrics should include repository growth, contributor growth, and skill adoption.
title: Implement Trending Engine

body:
## Context

All 7 crawler workflows have been disabled (`on: []`) in branch `chore/disable-crawlers` pending a full rework. PR #685 demonstrated that the current crawlers produce too much noise — 294 proposals of which ~86% were rejected on audit.

## Current Architecture

### Workflows (all disabled)
| Workflow | Schedule | Source | Script |
|---|---|---|---|
| `crawl-github.yml` | Friday 6am UTC | GitHub topic search | `scripts/crawlers/github/crawler.py` |
| `crawl-hf.yml` | Thursday 6am UTC | HuggingFace model API | `scripts/crawlers/huggingface/crawler.py` |
| `crawl-mcp.yml` | Monday 6am UTC | mcp.so + smithery.ai | `scripts/crawlers/mcp_registry/crawler.py` |
| `crawl-npm.yml` | Tuesday 6am UTC | npm keyword search | `scripts/crawlers/npm/crawler.py` |
| `crawl-papers.yml` | Sunday 6am UTC | Semantic Scholar + arXiv | `scripts/crawlers/papers/crawler.py` |
| `crawl-skillsmp.yml` | Saturday 6am UTC | skillsmp.com API | `scripts/crawlers/skillsmp/crawler.py` |
| `crawl-vscode.yml` | Wednesday 6am UTC | VS Code Marketplace | `scripts/crawlers/vscode_marketplace/crawler.py` |

### Shared Utilities (`scripts/crawlers/common/`)
| Module | What it does | Keep? |
|---|---|---|
| `taxonomy_mapper.py` | Maps keywords/topics → Gaia generic skill IDs | ✅ Useful core — needs expansion + accuracy fixes |
| `evidence_scorer.py` | Computes 0-100 quality score from marketplace signals (downloads, stars, recency, docs) | ✅ Good foundation — needs calibration |
| `candidate_builder.py` | Builds valid skill node candidates matching schema | ✅ Keep as-is |
| `dedup.py` | Deduplicates candidates against existing `registry/gaia.json` | ✅ Keep — needs named-skills.json dedup too |
| `proposer.py` | Writes proposal JSON batches to `proposals/` dir | ✅ Keep as-is |

### Config (`scripts/crawlers/config.yaml`)
Central config with per-source settings (keywords, thresholds, batch sizes). Useful structure, thresholds need tightening.

### Supporting Infrastructure
- `stale-bot.yml` — Auto-closes bot proposal PRs after 30+7 days. **Keep active.**
- `auto-label-bot-prs.yml` — Labels bot PRs. **Keep active.**
- `.agents/skills/gaia-bot-curate/` — Agent skill for curating `bot/*` branches. Needs update after rework.

## What Went Wrong (PR #685 Lessons)

### 1. HuggingFace crawler produced 193 bare model weight entries
- **Root cause**: No "agent interface" filter. Any model with >1k downloads was proposed.
- **Fix**: Require agent signal — SKILL.md, MCP manifest, AGENTS.md, or CLI tool in repo.

### 2. GitHub crawler produced 38 web scrapers
- **Root cause**: `browser-automation` topic mapped to `web-scrape` (wrong). Overly broad topics.
- **Fix**: Tighter topic→skill mapping. Require agent-facing README section or agent integration docs.

### 3. Taxonomy mapper has misclassification bugs
- `browser-automation` → `web-scrape` (should map to `browser-automation`)
- `image-generation` → `image-caption` (wrong)
- `workflow-orchestration` → `plan-decompose` (too broad)
- `multi-agent` → `multi-agent-debate` (too specific)

### 4. Evidence scoring is too generous
- `has_ci: True` hardcoded for all GitHub repos (line 97 of github/crawler.py)
- `has_readme: True` hardcoded (line 98)
- Score threshold of 30/100 is too low; repos with just 50 stars pass easily

### 5. No agent interface detection
- No check for SKILL.md, AGENTS.md, MCP manifest, or agent-specific docs
- This is the single biggest missing filter

### 6. No family deduplication
- 11 Qwen3 variants, 14 NLI classifiers, 14 SQuAD QA models proposed separately
- Needs: group by model family / org, propose only the canonical variant

### 7. Named-skill dedup is missing
- `dedup.py` only checks `registry/gaia.json` generic skills, not `registry/named-skills.json`
- Duplicates of existing named skills slip through

## Recommended Rework Plan

### Phase 1: Add Agent Interface Detection
Add a `has_agent_interface()` check that looks for:
- `SKILL.md` in repo root or `.claude/` or `.agents/`
- `AGENTS.md` or `CLAUDE.md`
- MCP server manifest (`mcp.json`, `package.json` with `"mcp"` field)
- README sections mentioning "agent", "MCP", "skill", "Claude Code", "Codex"

### Phase 2: Fix Taxonomy Mapper
- Fix misclassifications listed above
- Add new mappings for MCP-specific categories
- Add negative keywords (e.g., "download" → not web-scrape)

### Phase 3: Tighten Scoring & Thresholds
- Remove hardcoded `has_ci`/`has_readme` booleans — detect these from API
- Raise score threshold from 30 to 50
- Raise min stars from 50 to 200 for GitHub
- Add agent interface bonus: +20 points if SKILL.md detected

### Phase 4: Add Family Deduplication
- Group by `org/` prefix
- For HuggingFace: group by model family (e.g., all `qwen3-*` → one entry)
- Propose only the most popular variant per family

### Phase 5: Expand Named-Skill Dedup
- Check against `registry/named-skills.json` in addition to `gaia.json`
- Check for semantic duplicates (same repo, different slug)

### Phase 6: Source-Specific Improvements
- **HuggingFace**: Only crawl models that have associated Spaces, MCP servers, or SKILL.md
- **GitHub**: Add agent-specific topics: `mcp-server`, `claude-skill`, `agent-skill`, `codex-skill`
- **MCP Registry**: This source is the highest quality — expand batch size, lower thresholds
- **Papers**: Focus on papers that produced tools/repos, not pure theory

## Files Preserved
All scripts in `scripts/crawlers/` are preserved. Only the workflow triggers were removed.
Restore triggers by replacing `on: []` with the original `on: schedule: ...` block.
title: [Crawler Rework] Document current architecture and improvement plan

body:
# Infrastructure Simplification & CI Optimization

## Background
Following a review of the repository's infrastructure, CI workflows, and testing suite, we've identified several areas where we can reduce boilerplate, cut down CI execution times, and clean up technical debt. 

## Proposed Action Items

### 1. Consolidate Crawlers into a "Batch Crawl" Workflow
Currently, we have 7 separate crawler workflows (`crawl-github.yml`, `crawl-npm.yml`, etc.) staggered across different days of the week, each pushing to its own bot branch.
- **Action:** Consolidate these into a single `crawl-all.yml` workflow (using a matrix or sequential script). 
- **Goal:** Run all crawlers at once on a weekly schedule, pushing all proposals to a single unified `bot/batch-crawl/YYYY-MM-DD` branch. This reduces 200+ lines of duplicate YAML boilerplate and means we only need to review and approve **one batch crawl PR** per week instead of seven separate ones.

### 2. Eliminate Duplicate CI Test Execution
Currently, `python-package.yml` and `validate.yml` overlap on trigger paths (e.g., `tests/**`, `packages/**`), causing both to run `gaia test all` independently for the same commit.
- **Action:** Refactor the test execution. We can extract `gaia test all` into a shared Reusable Workflow (`workflow_call`) or adjust the path triggers so that the full test suite strictly runs once per commit. 
- **Goal:** Save 1-2 minutes of duplicated CI compute time and prevent double-reporting on pull requests.

### 3. Implement Parallel Testing (`pytest-xdist`)
Our test suite currently runs sequentially. Because our `conftest.py` already uses the `tmp_path` fixture to strictly isolate `GAIA_HOME` per test worker, our tests are inherently thread-safe.
- **Action:** Add `pytest-xdist` to the `dev` dependencies in `pyproject.toml` and configure `pytest.ini_options` to run tests using `-n auto`.
- **Goal:** Dramatically speed up local test execution and GitHub Actions CI turnaround (often a 2x-4x speedup on multi-core runners).

### 4. Prune / Refactor Obsolete PR-Specific Tests
We have several test files named after specific sandbox reviews (e.g., `test_pr540_review.py`, `test_pr635_review.py`). 
- **Action:** Audit these files. Move valid regression checks for general features (like `--custom` flags or scanner behavior) into their respective domain test files (`test_graph.py`, `test_scanner.py`). Delete the remaining one-off test files.
- **Goal:** Keep the test suite domain-oriented rather than history-oriented, making it easier to maintain.

### 5. Delete `tests/_archive/`
There is an archived tests directory which is already explicitly ignored by pytest.
- **Action:** Delete the `tests/_archive/` folder from the repository entirely. 
- **Goal:** Reduce repository footprint and prevent these files from cluttering global text searches or confusing AI agents. Git history will preserve them if they are ever needed.
title: Infrastructure Simplification & CI Optimization

body:
Phase 1 deliverable (v2 roadmap "Verification Workflow") — the last Phase 1 feature without an issue. Standalone (not a sub-issue); consumes the trust model decided on #646.

## Model

Verification levels are **predicates over signals we already ship or have specced** — no new scoring machinery. Definitions below are starting points for calibration (RFC).

| Level | Predicate (proposed) |
|---|---|
| Community Verified | ≥1 evidence entry with `verified: true` (4★ Verifier review) and none `disputed` |
| Security Reviewed | Passes the #185 security scan (no high-risk findings) |
| Benchmark Verified | Deferred to the benchmark framework (#649, design-only in Phase 1) — define the predicate now, implement later |
| Enterprise Ready | All of the above + Overall Trust Grade ≥ A + rank tenure ≥ 30 days (threshold adjustable later) |

## Notes

- Everything is **skill-level**, consistent with #646 — repositories only contribute evidence.
- Depends on: #646 (grade schema + Overall Trust Grade), #185 (scanner). Related: #650 (certification tiers consume these levels), #654 (evidence types).
- Levels are recomputed by the build pipeline, never hand-assigned (Programmatic-First).
- Display: level badges per skill; vocabulary checked against CONTEXT.md (no rarity references).

## Definition of Done

- [ ] Level predicates calibrated and documented
- [ ] Levels computed in generated catalogs
- [ ] Site displays level badges with the predicate explained ("why this level, what's missing for the next")
- [ ] Benchmark Verified predicate documented (implementation deferred to #649's framework)

_Created by the Orchestrator agent on Marco's behalf (approved 2026-06-10)._
title: Implement Verification Workflow — levels as predicates over Overall Trust Grade, tenure, and security scan

body:
Sub-issue of #646, spun out of the trust model decisions (2026-06-10).

## Context

The trust model separates two axes:

- **Evidence grades** — S / A / B / C (design colors Platinum / Gold / Silver / Bronze), derived from an underlying trust number. An **Overall Trust Grade** per skill accumulates the gathered evidence to establish, beyond reasonable doubt, that this is a good skill.
- **Evidence types** — what kind of artifact backs the claim. This RFC covers types only.

Previously the schema conflated the two: tier A = arxiv, tier B = repo.

## Current types

- arxiv
- repo
- GitHub stars (proxy)

## Candidates to evaluate

- Community posts about the skill (forums, blogs, social)
- YouTube / Instagram views as proxy evidence
- Other trust indicators — open to proposals

## Open questions

- [ ] How does each type feed the trust number behind the grade?
- [ ] Verification story for social metrics (spoofable, volatile)
- [ ] Staleness/decay rules per type
- [ ] Vocabulary check against CONTEXT.md before any copy ships

_Created by the Orchestrator agent on Marco's behalf (approved 2026-06-10)._
title: RFC: Evidence types — expand beyond arxiv / repo / stars

body: Currently, `scripts/generateProjections.py` generates `skill-tree.md` files that display the full skill tree for each user. However, this is incorrect. The generated tree should only display a narrow and precise path specific to the user's journey, rather than the entire taxonomy. This causes all user `skill-tree.md` files to look structurally identical instead of reflecting their unique paths.
title: Generated skill-tree.md shows full tree instead of narrow/precise path

body:
### Background
PR #635 introduced `--custom` scoping to `gaia scan`, `gaia tree`, and `gaia graph`. However, it implemented this by isolating custom skills into flat, detached structures that ignore the canonical tree logic. 

### What is currently done
- We have the ability to locate and parse local custom skills (`scan_skill_mds`).
- We have a semantic matcher (`match_skill_to_canonical`) that can determine if a custom skill overlaps with a canonical one.
- We have CLI flags `--custom` for `tree` and `graph`.

### What is yet to be done (The Intended Behavior)
1. **`gaia scan` mapping**: `gaia scan` should actively map custom skills against the canonical registry using semantic matching. If a custom skill is semantically similar to a canonical one, it should map to that canonical node (inheriting its rich lineage, level, and graph placement). If it's completely new, it should map as a brand new "starless" generic skill.
2. **`gaia graph --custom`**: Instead of building a detached, synthetic 0-star graph, it should overlay the custom skills on top of the canonical graph. This mimics the semantic graph on the website, integrating custom skills into the rich canonical paths.
3. **`gaia tree --custom`**: Instead of hiding all canonical skills and breaking continuity, it should show the tree *against* the canon graph. It should display the canonical lineages that lead to (or are replaced by) the custom skills.
title: Re-architect custom skill scoping to overlay against canonical graph

body:
### Description
The Gaia CLI is designed to be local-first, yet several non-dev commands default to displaying/using canonical or global registry data, requiring developers to pass explicit flags to see their own local/custom skills. To align the developer experience with the local-first ethos, these commands should be changed to default to the local custom skill context, with canonical/global views moved to opt-in flags.

### Audit of Non-Compliant Commands

1. **`gaia tree`**
   - *Current default*: Renders the canonical/unlocked skill tree.
   - *Proposed default*: Show local/custom skills in the repository context by default. Canonical view should require `--canon`.

2. **`gaia graph`**
   - *Current default*: Generates the full canonical registry graph.
   - *Proposed default*: Generate a graph containing only project-local custom skills and their prerequisites. Canonical graph should require `--canon`.

3. **`gaia stats`**
   - *Current default*: Renders stats for the canonical `gaia.json` graph, ignoring the `--canon` flag entirely.
   - *Proposed default*: Show stats for project-local custom skills and the local user tree. Support `--canon` to show canonical stats.

4. **`gaia lookup`**
   - *Current default*: Searches the canonical registry graph.
   - *Proposed default*: Search local custom skills and installed manifest by default. Add `--canon` to search the canonical registry graph.

5. **`gaia path`**
   - *Current default*: Computes prerequisites and paths using the canonical registry graph.
   - *Proposed default*: Compute unlock paths using the local custom skill graph by default. Add `--canon` to compute using the canonical registry.

6. **`gaia skills list` / `gaia skills search`**
   - *Current default*: Lists/searches remote/available named skills in the registry.
   - *Proposed default*: Default to listing/searching locally installed custom skills. Use `--available` or `--remote` to view remote skills.
title: [CLI] Default non-dev CLI commands to local-first / custom skill context

body:
### Description
Following recent WWDC / Apple developer tool intelligence updates (e.g., Xcode Swift Assist, local agent/AI instructions, and workspace configurations), we should add support for scanning Xcode-specific local agent skill/rule paths in the CLI scanner.

### Proposed Changes
In [scanner.py](file:///Users/marcotiongson/Documents/gaia-skill-tree/src/gaia_cli/scanner.py):
1. Add `.xcode/rules` and/or `.xcode/skills` to the project-local search candidates in `_skill_search_dirs()`.
2. Add `".xcode"` to the list of `allowed_prefixes` in `_should_prune_dir()`.

This will ensure Xcode-based developer environments and custom instructions/skills are correctly detected when running CLI commands like `gaia scan`.
title: Include Xcode skills/rules when scanning in CLI (WWDC Apple update)

body:
I am baffled, hermes for example has a lot of self-made skills.
Currently, by design since we don't store full skill mds for privacy (and reproducibility), there is no way for gaia push to push anything when environment is hermes.
However, gaia init + gaia scan works >>> which is problematic because it gives false hope that it will work outside a repo.


So how to go about it? We could just warn them that gaia push won't work outside a repo, but we could also make it work such that they can make trees or graphs for hermes skills just for fun.

Since you are a hermes user @rico-favor maybe you have any ideas for this.
title: gaia init - behavior when initializing in non-repo environments (example: hermes, openclaw)

body:
### Summary
Add an '--update' flag to 'gaia push' to support incremental skill pushes for repositories that have already been submitted for intake.

### Problem
Currently, 'gaia push' scans a repository and creates a new draft intake batch (via a GitHub issue) containing all detected skills. If a contributor adds new skills to a repository that has already been pushed, running 'gaia push' again may result in duplicate proposals or redundant review work. There is no built-in way to push *only* the new skills that aren't already pending review.

### Proposed Feature
1. **'--update' Flag**: Add a new flag to 'gaia push' that instructs the CLI to only push skills not present in previous batches from the same 'sourceRepo'.
2. **Auto-Detection**: If 'gaia push' detects that the repository has already been pushed (by checking for open issues with the 'draft-skills' label matching the 'sourceRepo'), it should automatically trigger the '--update' logic.
3. **Filtering**: The CLI should fetch (or track locally) which skills were previously pushed and filter them out of the 'proposedSkills' in the new batch.

### Example Workflow
1. User runs 'gaia push' for a repo with skills A, B, and C. Issue #1 is created.
2. User adds skill D to the repo.
3. User runs 'gaia push'.
4. CLI detects Issue #1 for this repo.
5. CLI automatically filters out A, B, and C, and pushes a new batch containing *only* skill D.

### Implementation Notes
- The CLI needs a way to query existing intake issues for the current 'sourceRepo'.
- 'src/gaia_cli/main.py's 'push_command' and 'src/gaia_cli/push.py's 'build_skill_batch' will need updates to handle this logic.
- We may need to store a 'last pushed batch' state in '.gaia/config.toml' or similar.

### Labels
'help wanted', 'enhancement'
title: gaia push: Add --update flag for incremental skill pushes

body:
### Summary
Currently, there is no direct command to demote or remove a skill from a local user's skill tree (`skill-trees/<username>/skill-tree.json`). If an operator or user needs to remove a skill (for instance, to undo an incorrect promotion or clean up legacy records), they must edit the JSON directly. This is undesirable because manual edits bypass the CLI's verification checks and do not write automated timeline entries.

### Suggested Solution
Introduce a new command/verb (or expand a sub-command) like `gaia demote <skillId> --user <username>` that:
1. Loads the specified user's skill tree (`skill-trees/<username>/skill-tree.json`).
2. Removes the skill from `unlockedSkills` or rolls back its level.
3. Automatically appends a `demote` action event to the user's `timeline` array.
4. Saves the user's skill tree and validates the result.

### Files to Modify
- `src/gaia_cli/main.py`: Register the new CLI command.
- `src/gaia_cli/promotion.py`: Implement the logic for tree pruning/demotion.
- Tests should be added under `tests/test_promotion.py`.
title: Implement CLI support for removing/demoting a skill from user trees

body:
Tiered proxy evidence is essential as it is very intuitive for people to understand in scale what it means.

This means:
- Github Stars
- Number of downloads

I would like not to prioritize this, rather supplement scientific and historical evidence.

This is tied to changes in the governance system. Right now, most of evidence linked is less scientific, since it's very hard to justify what "scientific" means--as Arxiv evidence lacks community trust. Having a tiered approach to proxy evidence is much easier to grasp, though again it will remain secondary.

Some tiered proxy evidence would be:
- Apex tier requiring 100k Repo stars
- Ultimate tier requiring 30k Repo stars
- 4 stars requiring 10k Repo stars
- 3 stars requiring 100 Repo stars

This will filter out most "new" skills, but also lays out a clear path of getting a 5 star or even a 6 star based on popularity, rather than hard evidence. Still thinking about whether this is the main one--leaning towards having this as a way to discern ceiling potentials rather than being the gate.
title: [IDEA] Proxy Evidence as secondary

body:
Looking for some more actual evidence for skill curation--not just "Github Stars" or other proxies, but real handler-linked evidence such as Historical expertise. Could be:
- conferences done by the handler
- real work done, tied to big tech
- real extensive knowledge shared through sites like YouTube, HuggingFace, etc.
- other evidence like books made, websites created, etc.

These can be real hard evidence that the developer themselves know about their work.

This is extra curation, so I would prioritize Ultimates and ultimate-candidates for this one.
title: [IDEA] Historical Evidence

body:
Currently there is only one theme on the website. Inspired by the day-night cycle, there should be a cycle wherein the whole site is in "Day" mode and "Night" mode, as well as some certain seasonal data such as solstices occur, there will be different OGs created as well--kind of like Starbucks cards where there are different types.

Therefore, a new "design system" system (I know, redundant) should be implemented where one can easily just add new design elements and "Switch" the website alongside it--making it seem alive and forever-changing.

This can be tied on to meta shifts happening, for example a PRUNING cycle indicates that skills are being "deleted" or demoted, while a CURATING cycle indicates that skills are being added or curated into the META. So, rebalancing has its own .

Then, special themes occur on a CROWNING cycle where a new ultimate is crowned--so the whole "site" celebrates--and this propagates onto the badges themselves.

Kind of fun and makes everything feel alive--though this will take a lot more maintenance than imagined.

I will hold onto this idea until maybe there are more people maintaining, or if AI can fully automate recreating new design systems based on the web content without deliberately changing functionality.
title: [IDEA] Adding design easter eggs, PRUNING and CURATING day/night cycle and CROWNING theme.

Parking lot for the upcoming naming meta. These 7 starless generics (added in #564 via `/gaia-curate-chain`) each have **Class B** evidence pointing at a real, verified GitHub `SKILL.md`. A Haiku agent re-verified every repo and SKILL.md path and pulled live star counts to serve as the basis for **Class B evidence + origin setting** when these get promoted to named skills later.

## Verification (all 7 repos exist, all SKILL.md paths resolve)

| Skill | Repo (owner/repo) | Repo | SKILL.md path | Stars | Range | Last push | Archived |
|-------|-------------------|------|---------------|------:|-------|-----------|----------|
| financial-modeling | anthropics/claude-cookbooks | ✅ | ✅ | 44,892 | **10k+** | 2026-05-30 | no |
| supply-chain-optimization | kishorkukreja/awesome-supply-chain | ✅ | ✅ | 24 | **<100** | 2026-05-28 | no |
| synthetic-data-generation | omer-metin/skills-for-antigravity | ✅ | ✅ | 88 | **<100** | 2026-01-22 | no |
| probabilistic-programming | K-Dense-AI/scientific-agent-skills | ✅ | ✅ | 27,116 | **10k+** | 2026-06-01 | no |
| causal-inference | aj-geddes/useful-ai-prompts | ✅ | ✅ | 245 | **100+** | 2026-03-04 | no |
| regulatory-compliance-mapping | dralgorhythm/claude-agentic-framework | ✅ | ✅ | 96 | **<100** | 2026-03-12 | no |
| edge-optimization | majiayu000/claude-skill-registry-data | ✅ | ✅ | 8 | **<100** | 2026-06-03 | no |

## Proposed named drafts (for the naming meta)

| Generic | Proposed named id | Origin repo | Star range | SKILL.md source |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|-----------:|-----------------|
| financial-modeling | `anthropics/creating-financial-models` | https://github.com/anthropics/claude-cookbooks | 10k+ | [SKILL.md](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-cookbooks/blob/main/skills/custom_skills/creating-financial-models/SKILL.md) |
| supply-chain-optimization | `kishorkukreja/inventory-optimization` | https://github.com/kishorkukreja/awesome-supply-chain | <100 | [SKILL.md](https://github.com/kishorkukreja/awesome-supply-chain/blob/main/skills/inventory-optimization/SKILL.md) |
| synthetic-data-generation | `omer-metin/synthetic-data` | https://github.com/omer-metin/skills-for-antigravity | <100 | [SKILL.md](https://github.com/omer-metin/skills-for-antigravity/blob/main/skills/synthetic-data/SKILL.md) |
| probabilistic-programming | `k-dense-ai/pymc` | https://github.com/K-Dense-AI/scientific-agent-skills | 10k+ | [SKILL.md](https://github.com/K-Dense-AI/scientific-agent-skills/blob/main/skills/pymc/SKILL.md) |
| causal-inference | `aj-geddes/causal-inference` | https://github.com/aj-geddes/useful-ai-prompts | 100+ | [SKILL.md](https://github.com/aj-geddes/useful-ai-prompts/blob/main/skills/causal-inference/SKILL.md) |
| regulatory-compliance-mapping | `dralgorhythm/compliance` | https://github.com/dralgorhythm/claude-agentic-framework | <100 | [SKILL.md](https://github.com/dralgorhythm/claude-agentic-framework/blob/main/.claude/skills/security/compliance/SKILL.md) |
| edge-optimization | `majiayu000/edge-model-compression` | https://github.com/majiayu000/claude-skill-registry-data | <100 | [SKILL.md](https://github.com/majiayu000/claude-skill-registry-data/blob/main/ai-ml/edge-model-compression/SKILL.md) |

## Notes / flags for the naming pass
- **Strong origins (10k+):** `anthropics/claude-cookbooks` (44.9k) and `K-Dense-AI/scientific-agent-skills` (27.1k) — best Class B anchors; safe to set origin to the repo.
- **Low-star but legitimate (<100):** supply-chain, synthetic-data, compliance, edge-optimization — real and recent, but thin signal; worth a second look before assigning higher stars to any named form.
- No archived repos; all pushed within the last ~12 months (`omer-metin/skills-for-antigravity` is the oldest at 2026-01-22).
- `links.github` for each named draft should reuse the exact `blob/<branch>/<subpath>` SKILL.md URL above (installer requires `blob/`, not `tree/`).
- Star ranges here are the **basis for origin + Class B**, not a star (maturity) assignment — leave the eventual named star level to the naming meta.

Parking this so it can be picked up when the naming meta lands. Source: #564.

body:
## Context

Sub-task of #455 (Github Badges).

The badge validation Pages Function shipped in `design/profile-enhancements-rev` (`functions/badges/[handle]/[file].js`) currently emits one structured JSON log line per request via `console.log` — **Path A** of the analytics plan discussed in `docs/CLOUDFLARE-SETUP.md`.

That gives us real-time tailing (`wrangler pages deployment tail` + jq) but **no historical queryability**: Cloudflare's Workers Logs free tier rolls events off after ~7 days. For "how is badge adoption trending over a quarter?" we need a proper time-series store.

This issue tracks **Path B**: Cloudflare Workers Analytics Engine + a small admin dashboard.

## Proposed work

### 1. Wire the Analytics Engine binding

Add to `wrangler.toml`:

```toml
[[analytics_engine_datasets]]
binding = "BADGE_ANALYTICS"
dataset = "gaia_badges"
```

In `functions/badges/[handle]/[file].js`, alongside the existing `logEvent()`:

```js
env.BADGE_ANALYTICS?.writeDataPoint({
  blobs: [handle, file, repo || "_none_", state],   // string dimensions (max 6)
  doubles: [1],                                     // numeric metrics (max 6)
  indexes: [handle],                                // sampled-at index (max 1)
});
```

Fire-and-forget — no I/O wait on the request path. Free tier: 25M writes/day. Even at 1000 badge loads/min average we'd be at ~1.4M/day — well within the free quota.

### 2. Build an admin dashboard

New page at `docs/admin/badge-stats.html` + a Pages Function at `functions/admin/badge-stats.js` that:

- Reads a server-side Cloudflare GraphQL Analytics API token (env binding, not in code).
- Proxies queries against the `gaia_badges` dataset.
- Returns aggregated JSON for the page to render.

Page surfaces three views:

- **Top spoof attempts** — `state: validating` rows sorted by count, grouped by `repo_claimed`. Surfaces e.g. "evil-corp/some-repo tried to claim mattpocock's badge 1k times last week".
- **Most-embedded contributors** — `state: valid` grouped by `handle`, ordered descending. Bragging-rights metric + adoption signal.
- **Adoption curve** — `state: valid` summed by week. Shows whether Path A onboarding is converting.

Auth: gate with the same OAuth that #155 will land (only repo maintainers can access). Until then, hide behind a basic-auth secret so the URL isn't public.

### 3. Document the schema

Add to `docs/CLOUDFLARE-SETUP.md` § Analytics:

- Dataset schema: `blobs[0..3]`, `doubles[0]`, `indexes[0]`.
- Sample GraphQL query for "top 10 (handle, repo, state) tuples last 7 days".
- How to extend the schema without breaking the dashboard (column ordering matters in Analytics Engine).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Pages Function writes one data point per badge request to `gaia_badges` dataset.
- [ ] `wrangler.toml` has the dataset binding committed.
- [ ] Admin dashboard at `/admin/badge-stats` renders the three views and is gated.
- [ ] GraphQL query examples documented in `docs/CLOUDFLARE-SETUP.md`.
- [ ] Free-tier quota check: confirm we're under 25M writes/day at peak.
- [ ] Auto-deploy works (no manual Cloudflare console changes after first dataset creation).

## Out of scope

- Per-token analytics (rendering, expiry-soon warnings) — depends on #494 (server-rendered token-bound badges) shipping first.
- User-facing per-handle analytics ("see how often your badge is loaded") — separate UX surface, not part of this issue.

---

Filed from the `design/profile-enhancements-rev` discussion. Path A (structured `console.log`) is already live; this issue is just for the historical-queryable upgrade.
title: [badges] Workers Analytics Engine + admin dashboard for badge metrics (sub-task of #455)

body:
## Context

Sub-task of #155 (NEW UI — sign in to github).

Today's badge validation (shipped in `design/profile-enhancements-rev`) is **honor-system**: a Cloudflare Pages Function checks `?repo=<owner>/<repo>` against `docs/badges/registry.json` and serves a "validating" placeholder when it doesn't match. This catches casual copy-paste theft but is trivially bypassed by anyone who edits the URL string — no Referer is sent (camo strips it), so we have no way to prove the README *actually* lives on the claimed repo.

This issue tracks the proper fix: **server-rendered, OAuth-token-bound badges**. Blocked on #155 landing first.

## Proposed flow (once #155 ships GitHub OAuth)

1. **Token issuance.** User logs in with GitHub OAuth → visits `gaiaskilltree.com/badges/?u=<handle>` → clicks "Generate badge for `<owner>/<repo>`". Server-side function:
   - Verifies the OAuth session belongs to `<handle>`.
   - Verifies the user has `repo` scope (or push access via `gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>` while authenticated as them).
   - Mints a JWT signed with a Cloudflare-side secret: `{handle, repo, iat, exp}`. Expiry ~1 year.
2. **Embedded URL.** Page hands user a markdown snippet like `![](https://gaiaskilltree.com/badges/render?token=<jwt>)`. Token is opaque — they can copy the URL but can't construct one for someone else's repo without the signing secret.
3. **Render-time validation.** Each request to `/badges/render` decodes the JWT, verifies the HMAC, checks expiry, then renders the badge inline (SVG string-templating; PNG only if needed). Token mismatch → validating placeholder, same as today.
4. **Re-issuance UX.** When tokens approach expiry, the badges page surfaces a "Refresh your badge" CTA on next visit. README needs updating once a year.
5. **Revocation.** A Cloudflare KV store of `revoked_tokens` keyed by JWT `jti`. Function checks on every request. Lets us kill compromised tokens without rotating the master signing secret.

## What this gets us

- A copied URL still shows the original handle's badge — so it's no longer "stealing identity", it's "displaying the original owner's badge". Impersonation becomes structurally impossible without the signing secret.
- Per-token analytics (issuance time, last-rendered, expiry) come for free via the JWT payload.
- The rendering endpoint can also short-circuit dynamic state (e.g., live rank changes between regeneration cycles) since it runs per-request.

## What this costs (why it's not a quick follow-up)

- **GitHub OAuth integration** — a new OAuth app, login button, callback URL, session storage. Out of scope until #155 lands.
- **Signing secret** — stored in Cloudflare environment bindings (not in code). New operational burden: rotation, leak response.
- **Per-request CPU** — SVG templating is fine in a Worker; PNG rasterization (`@vercel/og`, `satori`) approaches the free-tier CPU-time ceiling. Decide which formats are worth the cost.
- **Revocation list** — KV reads add ~5–10ms per request. Cache aggressively.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] OAuth login (#155) is live and binds a session to a GitHub handle.
- [ ] New endpoint `POST /badges/issue` mints a signed JWT for an authenticated `(handle, repo)` pair.
- [ ] New endpoint `GET /badges/render?token=<jwt>` decodes, verifies, and serves the badge SVG inline.
- [ ] Existing static badges (`/badges/<handle>/<file>.svg`) remain functional for back-compat.
- [ ] Page UI offers a toggle: "Use signed badge (recommended) vs. static badge" so contributors can opt in incrementally.
- [ ] `docs/CLOUDFLARE-SETUP.md` documents secret-rotation procedure and how to revoke a token.
- [ ] Documented in `CONTRIBUTING.md` § Badges so new contributors understand the flow.

## Out of scope

- Switching all existing `<handle>/<file>.svg` URLs to signed form. The static path stays as a fallback indefinitely.
- Per-repo analytics dashboard — tracked separately under the #455 Path B sub-task.

---

Filed from the `design/profile-enhancements-rev` discussion. See `docs/CLOUDFLARE-SETUP.md` § "Open questions" for the threat-model rationale.
title: [badges] Server-rendered, OAuth-token-bound badge endpoint (sub-task of #155)

body:
Following the May 2026 Registry Audit, the following recommendations are proposed to automate and harden the registry maintenance process:

### 1. Automated CI Gating
- **Action**: Integrate `scripts/verify_evidence.py` into the CI pipeline (pre-commit or GitHub Actions).
- **Goal**: Prevent PRs from introducing dead links and ensure continuous liveness of the registry.

### 2. Evidence "Freshness" & Decay Demerits
- **Action**: Add a `lastChecked` timestamp to the evidence schema.
- **Rule**: Implement a demerit for evidence that has rotted (not verified for >6 months), encouraging periodic re-validation.

### 3. Multi-Source Verification Bar
- **Action**: Require at least two distinct Class B/C sources for any skill reaching **4★ (Hardened)** or higher.
- **Goal**: Minimize reliance on single repositories for high-prestige nodes.

### 4. Programmatic Structural Integrity
- **Action**: Automate the reclassification of `Unique` skills to `Basic`/`Extra` when demoted below the 4★ threshold.
- **Goal**: Maintain DAG validity without manual intervention during audit demotions.

### 5. Verified/Disputed Metadata
- **Action**: Fully implement the `verified` and `disputed` flags in the registry schema to support the "Web of Trust" model.

These items will ensure Gaia remains the high-prestige, high-trust standard for agentic capabilities.
title: RFC: Meta-Audit Automation & Evidence Integrity Roadmap

body:
`packages/mcp/src/config/identity.ts` resolves `.gaia/config.json` relative to `process.cwd()`. When an MCP host (e.g. Claude Code) launches the server, the working directory may not be the repo root.

**Impact:** `gaia_my_tree` and `gaia_propose` fall back to `~/.gaia/config.json` or return a 'no user configured' error even when a project-level config exists.

**Fix:** Set `GAIA_USER` in `.mcp.json`'s `env` block as the reliable override, and/or resolve the config path from the script's own `import.meta.url` rather than `cwd`.
title: mcp: identity resolution uses process.cwd() — breaks when server CWD is not repo root

body:
## Motivation

The registry is growing. Right now there is no formal document defining who can merge what, how disputes are resolved, what quorum is needed for different change types, or how maintainers are added and removed. Implicit governance works at small scale; it breaks down as contributors and stakes increase.

This RFC is not about code. It is about formalising the social contract that protects the integrity of `gaia.json`.

---

## Scope of decisions the governance model must cover

1. **Merge authority**: who can approve and merge PRs of each type (atomic, composite, legendary, reclassify, named, user-tree)?
2. **Dispute resolution**: what happens when two maintainers disagree on a proposed skill?
3. **Maintainer lifecycle**: how are maintainers nominated, onboarded, and removed?
4. **Evidence disputes**: what happens when a maintainer challenges the validity of evidence on an existing skill?
5. **Emergency response**: who can revert a merge that introduced a bug or bad data?

---

## Open questions

### Q1 — Maintainer tier structure

- **A. Flat**: all maintainers have equal authority over all change types
- **B. Two-tier**: regular maintainers can approve atomic/composite/named PRs; a smaller group of core maintainers is needed for legendary/reclassify/schema changes
- **C. Domain-based**: maintainers self-assign to domains (NLP skills, agent orchestration skills, etc.) and have primary authority over those areas only

### Q2 — Quorum requirements by change type

What minimum approvals should be required before merge?

| Change type | Proposed minimum |
|---|---|
| User tree (`users/`) | 0 (CI-gated only) |
| Named skill (`graph/named/`) | 1 maintainer |
| Atomic skill (new) | 1 maintainer |
| Composite skill (new) | 2 maintainers |
| Legendary skill (new or promote) | 2 maintainers + 2 Class A/B evidence sources |
| Reclassify existing skill | 2 maintainers |
| Schema change | All core maintainers (unanimous or supermajority?) |
| Skill deprecation or retirement | 2 maintainers + community comment period |

Is this table right? What changes?

### Q3 — Dispute resolution

When maintainers disagree:
- **A. Majority vote** among active maintainers (defined as having merged a PR in the last 90 days)
- **B. Defer to original proposer + waiting period**: if no consensus in 14 days, the proposal is shelved
- **C. Core maintainer veto**: any core maintainer can block a merge; requires a second core maintainer to override
- **D. Public comment period**: contested changes are opened to community comment for 7 days before a final vote

### Q4 — Maintainer inactivity

A maintainer who has not reviewed or merged anything in 180 days:
- Automatically moves to emeritus status?
- Receives a ping and is removed if no response in 30 days?
- Retains full authority indefinitely?

### Q5 — Where does governance live?

- A new `GOVERNANCE.md` file in the repo root (already exists as a stub — fill it in)
- Embedded in `CLAUDE.md` (risks making CLAUDE.md too long)
- A separate `docs/governance.md` linked from the README

---

## Constraints already decided

- The Legendary skill tier already requires 2 maintainer approvals and `status: "validated"` at merge — any governance model must be consistent with this.
- `GOVERNANCE.md` already exists as a stub in `docs/` — the outcome of this RFC should populate it.

---

## What to do

Comment with your preferred answers. A concrete draft of the quorum table (Q2) with your amendments is the most immediately actionable contribution. If you are willing to draft `GOVERNANCE.md` based on the consensus that emerges, say so — that is the deliverable this RFC is aiming for.
title: RFC: governance model — who has authority over the canonical graph?

body:
## Motivation

17 skills in `gaia.json` have a `conditions` field, but it is a plain string today:

```json
"conditions": "Structured output mode required."
```

This means conditions are human-readable but not machine-checkable. A scanner or validator cannot determine whether a user's environment satisfies the condition. The field promises semantic meaning but delivers none.

---

## What conditions actually express

Looking at the 17 existing entries, conditions fall into roughly four categories:

| Category | Example |
|---|---|
| Runtime requirement | "Requires a tool-calling-capable model" |
| Input format requirement | "Structured output mode required" |
| Access / permission | "Requires internet access" |
| Composability constraint | "Must be combined with a retrieval skill" |

A structured language should be able to express all four.

---

## Open questions

### Q1 — Representation format

- **A. Key-value pairs**: `{"requires": ["tool-calling", "internet"], "format": "structured-output"}` — simple, extensible, no new syntax to learn
- **B. Boolean expression string**: `"tool-calling AND (internet OR local-kb)"` — expressive but requires a parser
- **C. Array of typed constraint objects**: `[{"type": "model-capability", "value": "tool-calling"}, {"type": "access", "value": "internet"}]` — verbose but maximally structured
- **D. Keep free-text, add a separate `constraintTags` array** — non-breaking; free-text stays for humans, tags are for machines

### Q2 — Vocabulary: who defines valid constraint values?

- A closed enum in `skill.schema.json` (e.g. `"model-capability"`, `"access"`, `"format"`, `"prerequisite-skill"`)
- An open string namespace (contributors define their own, validator warns on unknowns)
- A separate `graph/constraints.json` vocabulary file that grows over time

### Q3 — Validation behaviour

When a condition is not met (i.e. the scan environment does not satisfy a constraint):
- **A. Error**: the skill is excluded from fusion candidates entirely
- **B. Warning**: the skill appears as a candidate but is flagged `"conditions_unmet": true`
- **C. Advisory only**: conditions are never checked programmatically, only shown to the user

### Q4 — Migration of the 17 existing entries

Converting 17 free-text strings to structured constraints requires human judgement (the strings are not parseable). Options:
- **A. Migrate in the same PR** that adds the new schema — required before merge
- **B. Two-phase**: accept both formats during a transition window; `validate.py` warns on free-text; free-text removed in a later cleanup PR
- **C. No migration required**: free-text becomes a `description` subfield within the structured format; existing data is valid as `{"description": "..."}` with no machine-checkable constraints

### Q5 — Interaction with `gaia scan`

Should `gaia scan` read the user's `.gaia/config.json` for declared capabilities (e.g. `"capabilities": ["tool-calling", "internet"]`) and cross-check against skill conditions before surfacing a fusion candidate?

---

## Constraints already decided

- `conditions` is already in `skill.schema.json` — any proposal must be schema-compatible or include a migration.
- `generateProjections.py` renders conditions into `.md` files — the renderer must be updated to handle the new format.
- Free-text conditions in the 17 existing skills must not be silently lost during migration.

---

## What to do

Comment with your preferred approach for Q1 (the representation format) — that is the most consequential choice and everything else follows from it. A concrete JSON example of how `web-scrape`'s condition would look in your proposed format is the most useful contribution.
title: RFC: skill conditions — upgrade the free-text field to a structured constraint language

body:
## Motivation

Skills in `gaia.json` already have a `version` field (currently `"0.1.0"` or `"0.2.0"`), but there is no defined policy for when to bump it, what constitutes a breaking change, or how user skill trees should respond to a version change. Without rules, version bumps are arbitrary and user trees silently drift.

---

## Open questions

### Q1 — What counts as a breaking change?

Proposed taxonomy:

| Change | Severity |
|---|---|
| Adding a new prerequisite | Breaking — existing holders may no longer qualify |
| Removing a prerequisite | Non-breaking |
| Raising the level floor | Breaking |
| Lowering the level floor | Non-breaking |
| Changing the description or name | Non-breaking |
| Changing `type` (e.g. atomic → composite) | Breaking |
| Adding or removing evidence | Non-breaking |

Is this taxonomy correct? What is missing?

### Q2 — Semver or simpler?

- **A. Full semver** (`major.minor.patch`): breaking = major bump. Aligns with software convention; may be overkill for a skill graph.
- **B. Integer generation** (`v1`, `v2`, `v3`): simpler; breaking and non-breaking changes both increment.
- **C. Date-stamped** (`2026-04-30`): no compatibility semantics, just a freshness indicator.

### Q3 — How are user trees affected by a breaking version bump?

When `web-scrape` goes from `v1` to `v2` (adding a new prerequisite):
- **A. Grandfathered**: users who hold `v1` keep it as a legacy entry. `v2` is a new acquisition.
- **B. Invalidated**: the skill entry is flagged `"stale": true` in the tree until the user re-qualifies.
- **C. Auto-migrated**: CI opens a PR against the user's tree file proposing the update.
- **D. No action**: version is metadata only; user trees are not affected.

### Q4 — Schema enforcement

Should `validate.py` enforce that a version bump accompanies any structural change (prerequisites, type, level)?
- If yes: what is the grace period before CI fails on a missing bump?
- If no: is version purely advisory?

### Q5 — Version history

Should the graph store the history of a skill's versions (a changelog), or only the current version?
- Storing history in `gaia.json` makes the file large.
- Storing history in git is free but requires `git log` to reconstruct.
- A separate `graph/changelog/` directory is a middle ground.

### Q6 — Interaction with named skills

A named skill is tied to a specific `genericSkillRef`. If the generic skill releases a breaking version:
- Should named skills declare which version of the generic skill they implement?
- Should named skills be re-validated against the new version?
- Is `level` on the named skill relative to the generic skill version?

---

## Constraints already decided

- `version` field exists in `skill.schema.json` — any proposal must be compatible with the current schema or include a migration path.
- `generateProjections.py` and `generateNamedIndex.py` must remain fast (no heavy changelog parsing at generation time).

---

## What to do

Comment with your preferred answers. A concrete proposal for the schema diff is especially helpful — even a single JSON snippet showing what a versioned skill entry looks like. Implementation will not begin until Q1, Q2, and Q3 have consensus.
title: RFC: skill versioning — what does it mean to upgrade a skill's definition?

body: ## Context Blocked on / follows from #70, which: - Tags each bucket entry in `graph/named/index.json` with `"role": "origin"` or `"role": "variant"` - Allows multiple named implementations to coexist under the same `genericSkillRef` Once that lands, both the CLI and the docs UI need to surface the full bucket — not just the origin entry. --- ## CLI: `gaia lookup ` There is currently no dedicated `lookup` command. Add one (or extend `gaia search`) that shows a skill's full detail including all named implementations in its bucket. ### Desired output ``` $ gaia lookup web-scrape web-scrape [composite · Level II · uncommon] Prerequisites: web-search, parse-html, extract-entities Named implementations (2) ★ origin karpathy/web-scrape Level III "Firecrawl-backed deep scraper" variant johndoe/web-scrape-stealth Level II "Stealth-mode scraper with rotation" Run `gaia install karpathy/web-scrape` to add an implementation to your project. ``` ### Rules - `★ origin` marker on the entry with `"role": "origin"` (or `origin: true` in frontmatter) - `variant` label for all other bucket entries - Each line shows: contributor/skill-name, level, and title if present - If no named implementations exist: print `No named implementations yet. Run \`gaia name\` to add one.` ### Implementation - New function `lookup_command` in `src/gaia_cli/main.py` - Reads `graph/gaia.json` for generic skill metadata - Reads `graph/named/index.json` bucket for named entries - Entry point: `gaia lookup ` --- ## Docs UI: skill card variant row The `renderCard` function in `docs/index.html` already has a **Variants** row, but it only renders variant IDs as plain chips: ```js variants.map(v => `${esc(v.id)}`) ``` ### Desired changes 1. **Show contributor attribution**: render each variant chip as `contributor/skill-name` not just the full ID. 2. **Show role**: the card being viewed should show `★ origin` in the header if it is the origin; variant cards should show `↳ variant of `. 3. **Variant chips link to their card**: clicking a variant chip in the card should scroll to and expand that variant's card. 4. **Graph node tooltip**: when the Named Skills HUD is active, hovering a node that has multiple bucket entries should show a count badge: `web-scrape (2 implementations)`. ### Implementation - All changes are in `docs/index.html` (self-contained page, no build step) - Use the `"role"` field added by #70 to `index.json` to drive origin/variant labelling - The scroll-to behaviour can use `document.querySelector('[data-id="..."]').scrollIntoView()` --- ## Acceptance criteria - [ ] `gaia lookup web-scrape` lists all bucket entries with role labels - [ ] `gaia lookup ` works for skills with no named implementations (prints guidance) - [ ] Variant row in docs skill card shows `contributor/skill-name` and links to the variant card - [ ] Origin card header shows `★ origin`; variant card header shows `↳ variant of ` - [ ] Graph node tooltip shows implementation count when bucket has > 1 entry - [ ] No regressions: single-entry buckets display correctly (no `variant` label on the sole entry)
title: Display all bucket variants in CLI `gaia lookup` and skill cards in docs UI

body:
## Motivation

The DAG in `gaia.json` encodes rich prerequisite chains — a legendary skill like `autonomous-research-agent` requires `research` → `ghostwrite` → `knowledge-harvest`, each of which has its own prerequisites. Right now there is no way to ask "what do I need to learn to unlock X?" without manually tracing the graph.

Two related gaps this issue covers:

1. **`gaia path <skill-id>`** — show the full unlock path from the user's current tree to a target skill.
2. **Combination path robustness** — `detect_combinations` in `combinator.py` treats prerequisites as a flat list. It does not handle multi-hop chains: a user might own the deep atomics for a legendary but the intermediary composites are not yet in their tree, so the fusion is silently missed.

---

## Feature 1: `gaia path <skill-id>`

```
$ gaia path autonomous-research-agent

autonomous-research-agent  [legendary]
├── research  [composite]  ← missing
│   ├── web-search  ✓ owned
│   ├── summarize  ✓ owned
│   └── cite-sources  ✗ missing
├── ghostwrite  [composite]  ← missing
│   ├── research  (see above)
│   ├── write-report  ✓ owned
│   └── audience-model  ✗ missing
└── knowledge-harvest  [composite]  ← missing
    ├── retrieve  ✓ owned
    ├── rerank  ✗ missing
    └── memory-management  ✗ missing

3 / 9 prerequisites owned.  6 skills needed to unlock.
```

Flags:
- `--owned-only` — show only the skills you still need (prune already-owned branches)
- `--json` — machine-readable output for scripting

## Feature 2: Multi-hop fusion detection

`combinator.py::detect_combinations` checks `all(prereq in combined_available for prereq in prereqs)`. This works for direct prerequisites but does not recurse.

**Current gap**: if a user owns `web-search`, `summarize`, `cite-sources` (the ingredients for `research`) but has not run `gaia fuse research` yet, `research` is not in `combined_available`, so `ghostwrite` and `autonomous-research-agent` are never surfaced as candidates — even though all the deep ingredients exist.

**Fix**: before checking composite/legendary prerequisites, run a transitive closure expansion — virtually "unlock" any composite whose deep ingredients are all present, even if the intermediate fusion step has not been confirmed yet. Surface these as `status: "chain_fusion"` candidates with a note listing the intermediate steps needed.

## Implementation notes

### `gaia path`

- New module: `src/gaia_cli/pathfinder.py`
- `def unlock_path(graph_data, target_id, owned_ids) -> PathNode` — recursive tree
- `def format_path(node, owned_ids, indent=0) -> str` — ASCII tree renderer
- Hook into `main.py` as `path_command`

### Multi-hop fusion

- Add `def transitive_close(graph_data, owned_ids) -> set[str]` to `combinator.py`
  - Walk composite/legendary skills; if all their prerequisites are in the expanding set, add them to it; repeat until stable
- Replace the `combined_available` set in `detect_combinations` with the transitive closure
- Tag results with `"status": "chain_fusion"` and `"chainSteps": [...]` listing intermediates

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `gaia path <skill-id>` prints a prerequisite tree with owned/missing markers
- [ ] `gaia path <skill-id> --owned-only` shows only missing skills
- [ ] `gaia path <skill-id> --json` outputs valid JSON
- [ ] Cyclic edge detection: `pathfinder.py` raises a clear error if the graph has a cycle (it should not, but defensive)
- [ ] `detect_combinations` surfaces chain-fusion candidates whose deep ingredients are all owned
- [ ] Chain-fusion candidates have `status: "chain_fusion"` and a `chainSteps` list
- [ ] Unit tests for `transitive_close` with a small fixture graph
- [ ] Unit tests for `unlock_path` covering: leaf node, single-hop composite, multi-hop legendary, partially owned tree

## Files to touch

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/gaia_cli/pathfinder.py` | New — path traversal + renderer |
| `src/gaia_cli/combinator.py` | Add `transitive_close`, update `detect_combinations` |
| `src/gaia_cli/main.py` | Add `path_command` and parser entry |
| `tests/` | Unit tests for both features |
title: CLI:  — show what it takes to unlock any skill

body:
## What to do

The registry currently has 100 skills. There are many real-world AI agent capabilities that are not represented yet.

If you use an AI agent for something that does not appear in [`registry.md`](registry.md), propose it here.

## How to propose

**Option A — quick proposal (comment on this issue)**

Reply with:
- Skill name (Title Case)
- Suggested ID (`kebab-case`)
- One-sentence description
- One public link demonstrating it (GitHub repo, paper, blog post)
- Type: `atomic`, `composite`, or `legendary`

We will batch accepted proposals into `gaia.json` after review.

**Option B — direct PR**

1. Add your skill to `graph/gaia.json` following the existing schema.
2. Run `python scripts/validate.py` — must pass.
3. Run `python scripts/generateProjections.py` to regenerate derived files.
4. Open a PR with title: `[atomic|composite|legendary] skill-id — brief rationale`

## What makes a good skill

- Represents a **capability** an AI agent can perform, not a specific tool or model
- Has at least one public, verifiable example
- Is distinct from skills already in the registry
- Uses a clear, concise `kebab-case` ID

## What to avoid

- Brand names as skill IDs (`gpt-4-call` → use `llm-inference`)
- Overly narrow or one-off capabilities
- Duplicates of existing skills under a different name
title: Propose a skill you use that is missing from the registry

body:
## Add yourself to the registry

Every contributor can have a personal skill tree at `users/<your-github-username>/skill-tree.json`.

Your skill tree records which Gaia skills you have developed — it is your public AI agent résumé.

## How to contribute

1. Fork the repo.
2. Copy `users/example/skill-tree.json` as a starting point.
3. Save it as `users/<your-github-username>/skill-tree.json`.
4. List the skill IDs you genuinely use. You can find all IDs in [`registry.md`](registry.md).
5. Open a PR — title: `[user-tree] your-username`

Validation runs automatically in CI (schema + reference check against `gaia.json`).

## Why bother?

- Your tree appears in the live skill-tree browser.
- The CLI `gaia graph` command visualises your personal skill graph.
- It is a low-effort, high-signal way to make your first contribution.

No code changes required — just JSON.
title: Submit your personal skill tree

body:
## Overview

`combinations.md` lists the current fusion recipe matrix, but as the atomic skill list grows, new composite combinations become possible that aren't yet in the graph.

## Your task

1. Read through `graph/gaia.json` — look at the atomic skills list
2. Identify a pair (or trio) of atomic skills that *should* produce a composite skill, but don't yet
3. Propose the new composite by editing `graph/gaia.json` with:
   - A unique `id`
   - `tier: composite`
   - `prerequisites: [skill-a-id, skill-b-id]`
   - A short `description`
   - Initial `rank: I` (Dormant)

## Good candidates to explore

- `code-generation` + `file-system-access` → something like "Scaffolding"?
- `web-search` + `fact-checking` → "Verified Research"?
- `embed-text` + `clustering` → "Semantic Grouping"?

Come up with your own — creativity welcome. See `combinations.md` for what already exists.

## How to contribute

1. Edit `graph/gaia.json`
2. `python3 scripts/validate.py`
3. `python3 scripts/generateProjections.py`
4. Open a PR describing why the combination makes sense
title: Add missing fusion recipes: what combinations are we missing?
